text: The patient is positioned supine on the operating table. Pneumoperitoneum is established using a Veress needle via the umbilicus, followed by the placement of an 8mm trocar in the peri-umbilical area. The abdominal cavity is inspected, revealing dense adhesions of the omentum and bowel to the anterior abdominal wall. Two additional robotic trocars are placed in the right abdomen and left upper abdomen, along with an assistant port in the left lower quadrant. The robotic system is docked from the left side.

The surgeon dissects and removes dense inflammatory adhesions in the upper abdomen to improve visualization. Due to the severity of the adhesions, the decision is made to abandon the attempt at performing a cholecystectomy.

The surgeon visualizes part of the body of the stomach and clears it to provide adequate space for the procedure. A gastrostomy is created to open the stomach and inspect the lumen, revealing a previously placed endoscopic stent with debris clogging it. The debris is cleared, and the stents are removed. The gastrostomy tract is expanded to open it further.

The surgeon encounters brisk bleeding while gradually incising through the stomach wall, initially unable to control it with a bipolar device. Pressure is applied to establish temporary control, and the bleeding is managed with two figure-of-eight sutures. The cystogastrotomy is expanded, and its edges are reinforced with two silk sutures, followed by securing the edges with a running PDA suture.

The surgeon washes out the cavity and removes necrotic material from the cyst during the necrosectomy. The anterior gastrotomy is closed using a running suture in a canal fashion, with an estimated blood loss of 300 mL.
